examples.title: Internet Checksum Calculation
examples.description: Algorithm to calculate Internet checksum as per RFC 1071 using one's complement arithmetic
examples.code:
FUNCTION Calculate_Checksum(Data[], Length)
 // Initialize 32-bit accumulator
 Sum ← 0
 
 // Process data as 16-bit words
 FOR i ← 0 TO Length-1 STEP 2
 // Form 16-bit word from two bytes
 Word ← (Data[i] << 8) + Data[i+1]
 Sum ← Sum + Word
 END FOR
 
 // Handle overflow (end-around carry)
 WHILE (Sum > 0xFFFF)
 Carry ← Sum >> 16 // Upper 16 bits
 Sum ← (Sum & 0xFFFF) + Carry
 END WHILE
 
 // One's complement of sum
 Checksum ← NOT Sum
 RETURN Checksum
END FUNCTION
examples.explanation: The algorithm processes data as 16-bit words, accumulates using one's complement addition (handling end-around carry), then takes one's complement of the final sum to get the checksum value.

examples.title: Checksum Verification
examples.description: Algorithm to verify received data using checksum
examples.code:
FUNCTION Verify_Checksum(Data[], Length, Received_Checksum)
 // Initialize accumulator
 Sum ← 0
 
 // Add all 16-bit words from data
 FOR i ← 0 TO Length-1 STEP 2
 Word ← (Data[i] << 8) + Data[i+1]
 Sum ← Sum + Word
 END FOR
 
 // Add the received checksum word
 Sum ← Sum + Received_Checksum
 
 // Handle overflow
 WHILE (Sum > 0xFFFF)
 Carry ← Sum >> 16
 Sum ← (Sum & 0xFFFF) + Carry
 END WHILE
 
 // If result is all 1s, no error
 IF Sum = 0xFFFF THEN
 RETURN TRUE // No error detected
 ELSE
 RETURN FALSE // Error detected
 END IF
END FUNCTION
examples.explanation: Verification adds the received checksum to the data words. If no errors occurred, the one's complement sum will equal all 1s (0xFFFF), indicating valid data.

examples.title: Simple Parity Check
examples.description: Simple single-bit parity generation for comparison with checksum
examples.code:
FUNCTION Generate_Parity(Data[], Length)
 Parity ← 0
 
 FOR i ← 0 TO Length-1
 // XOR each bit to count 1s
 FOR j ← 0 TO 7
 Parity ← Parity XOR (Data[i] >> j) & 1
 END FOR
 END FOR
 
 RETURN Parity // 0 for even parity, 1 for odd parity
END FUNCTION

FUNCTION Check_Parity(Data[], Length, Parity)
 Computed ← Generate_Parity(Data, Length)
 IF Computed = Parity THEN
 RETURN TRUE // No single-bit error
 ELSE
 RETURN FALSE // Error detected
 END IF
END FUNCTION
examples.explanation: Simple parity uses single bit to check odd/even number of 1s in data. Can detect only odd number of bit errors, unlike checksum which detects more error patterns.
